title: Migrate Order anemic data model to a rich Order aggregate enforcing state-transition invariants
business_context: A mid-market e-commerce platform processes ~85k orders/day across web and mobile. The Order entity is a bag of public setters mutated from 23 different service methods, and a race in the checkout flow has been double-charging ~40 customers/week because status transitions are validated ad-hoc in scattered services. Finance spends ~6 hours/week issuing refunds.
constraints: Zero downtime; the orders table holds 140M rows and cannot be locked for migration
Backward-compatible REST API (v1) must keep returning the flat order JSON for 9 mobile app versions still in the wild
Status transitions must be atomic under concurrent webhook + user actions (payment webhook and user cancel can arrive within 50ms)
Single Postgres primary with 4 read replicas; aggregate writes must go to primary only
No new external dependencies in the hot path (p99 checkout < 400ms)

before_state.description: Order is an anemic ORM row with public setters. Business rules (can a SHIPPED order be cancelled? can a paid order be re-paid?) live in OrderService, PaymentWebhookHandler, and AdminController independently, and they disagree.

issues: status is a free string; no enforcement of legal transitions
setStatus('paid') can be called twice -> double charge downstream
paidAt can be set without status moving to paid, leaving inconsistent rows
read-modify-write with no optimistic lock; concurrent webhook retries double-apply
no guard that order was in 'created' before paying
admin can cancel a shipped order, breaking fulfillment reconciliation
no domain event emitted, so inventory is never released

reasoning: Naive approach: add an if-check inside each of the 23 call sites to validate the transition. Rejected because the rules would still be duplicated and will drift again, and there is no atomicity guarantee under concurrency. Considered a database CHECK constraint + trigger for transitions, but rejected: triggers can't emit domain events, are hard to unit test, and obscure logic from the app team. Chosen path: introduce a rich Order aggregate whose only mutators are intention-revealing methods (markPaid, ship, cancel) that internally validate a transition table and append uncommitted domain events; persistence uses an optimistic version column. Cross-layer trace: UI/admin calls v1 controller -> controller loads aggregate via repository -> calls order.cancel() which throws InvalidTransition for shipped orders -> on success repo persists with WHERE version = $expected (atomic) and publishes OrderCancelled to the outbox -> async outbox worker releases inventory -> config flag rich_order_aggregate gates the new path -> contract tests pin the flat v1 JSON shape -> ramp from 1% to 100%.

after_state.description: Order is a rich aggregate. All mutations go through methods that validate a single source-of-truth transition table, bump an internal version, and record domain events. Repository persists atomically via optimistic concurrency and writes events to an outbox in the same transaction.

files.purpose: Single rich aggregate that makes illegal status transitions unrepresentable and records domain events; idempotent markPaid neutralizes webhook double-delivery.
Typed domain error so the API layer can map illegal transitions to HTTP 409 instead of 500.
Atomic persistence: optimistic version guard prevents lost updates, and events land in the outbox in the same transaction (no dual-write gap).
Flag-gated v1 controller that keeps the legacy flat JSON contract while routing through the rich aggregate.

migration_strategy: Add version (default 0) and outbox table via online migration (NOT NULL DEFAULT, no table rewrite); backfill version lazily on first write
Deploy rich aggregate behind flag rich_order_aggregate=off; legacy path still primary
Enable flag for internal tenant + 1% of traffic (canary); compare emitted events vs legacy side effects in shadow log
Ramp 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% over 5 days watching 409 rate and double-charge metric
Delete the 23 ad-hoc setStatus call sites and the public setters from the ORM model
Remove the flag and legacy path after 2 weeks at 100% with zero regressions
testing_strategy: Fixture: an order in 'created' with version=3. Test markPaid twice (simulating webhook retry) and assert status='paid' once, exactly one OrderPaid row in outbox, version incremented once. Concurrency test: two parallel save() calls on the same loaded aggregate -> assert exactly one COMMIT succeeds and the other throws ConcurrencyError (assert DB row updated once). Negative: cancel() on a 'shipped' order asserts InvalidTransition thrown and zero outbox rows. Chaos: kill the connection between the UPDATE and the outbox INSERT and assert ROLLBACK leaves status unchanged (no event without state change).
what_breaks_naive_approach: The per-call-site if-check leaves the read-modify-write non-atomic: two concurrent payment webhook retries both read status='created', both pass the check, both write 'paid', and the downstream charge fires twice. Only the optimistic version guard in the same transaction as the outbox write closes that window.

title: Extract a Money value object to kill scattered float arithmetic in a fintech ledger
business_context: A B2B payments startup runs a double-entry ledger handling ~$12M/day. Amounts are stored and manipulated as JS numbers (floats), and a rounding drift of fractions of a cent across 30M postings has produced a $4,200 unreconciled gap that auditors flagged. Engineers add ad-hoc Math.round calls inconsistently.
constraints: Ledger must remain double-entry balanced to the cent at all times (sum of debits == sum of credits)
Multi-currency: USD, EUR, GBP, JPY (0 decimal places) must all be supported correctly
Backward-compatible: existing ledger_entries.amount column is numeric(18,2); cannot change column type without a 30M-row rewrite
Calculations run in both the Node API and a Python reconciliation job; the value semantics must match across languages
No floating point in any new code path

before_state.description: Amounts are floats. Addition, fee splitting, and currency formatting each round differently, so a 3-way split of $10.00 yields 3.33+3.33+3.33=9.99 and the lost cent silently disappears.

issues: splitFee drops the remainder cent; postings don't sum to total
float add accumulates error across millions of postings
no currency dimension; JPY treated as 2-decimal
hardcodes 2 decimals, wrong for zero-decimal currencies
format and math disagree on rounding

reasoning: Naive approach: sprinkle more Math.round and a global EPSILON comparison. Rejected because rounding order still varies per call site and the remainder cent is never assigned to anyone, so the ledger stays unbalanced. Considered storing everything as a Decimal library object everywhere, but rejected as the only change: decimals alone don't encode currency or enforce same-currency arithmetic, so you can still add USD to JPY. Chosen path: a Money value object storing integer minor units + currency code, immutable, with an allocate() method that distributes remainders deterministically (largest-remainder). Cross-layer trace: API receives decimal string -> Money.fromDecimal parses to minor units by currency exponent -> domain math uses Money.add/allocate (integer only) -> repository serializes back to numeric(18,2) for storage (compat) but keeps minor-unit truth -> the Python recon job uses an identical Money port -> property tests assert allocate sums to the original.

after_state.description: Money is an immutable value object of (amountMinor: bigint, currency). All arithmetic is integer; allocate distributes remainder cents deterministically so splits always sum back. Currency exponents drive parsing and formatting.

files.purpose: Immutable integer-minor-unit money that makes float drift impossible and guarantees allocate() sums back to the original to the cent.
Formatting derives decimals from the currency exponent, fixing JPY and aligning display with stored math.
Fee splitting now uses allocate, so the three postings always sum to the original total with no orphaned cent.

migration_strategy: Introduce Money VO with no behavior change; add fromDecimal/toDecimal adapters at the repository boundary only
Route new postings through Money behind flag money_vo; storage column unchanged (still numeric(18,2))
Run the Python recon job in shadow comparing Money-computed splits vs legacy float splits, log diffs
Ramp to 100% once shadow diff is zero for 7 days; the $4,200 gap stops growing
Replace all remaining float arithmetic call sites with Money and delete add(number,number)
Backfill historical drift via a one-time correcting journal entry approved by finance
testing_strategy: Property test: for random total in 0..10^9 minor units and ways in 1..97, assert sum(allocate(total,ways)) === total exactly (no lost cent). Fixture: $10.00 split 3 ways -> [3.34,3.33,3.33] and assert sum=10.00. Cross-language test: feed the same 1000 vectors to the Node and Python Money ports and assert identical outputs. Negative: add(USD, JPY) throws 'currency mismatch'. Chaos: parse '0.005' under JPY exponent 0 and assert it truncates deterministically rather than throwing a float.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding more Math.round keeps each split rounded independently, so 10.00/3 stays 3.33*3=9.99 and the missing cent is never posted to any account, leaving double-entry permanently unbalanced. Only allocate()'s deterministic remainder distribution assigns every minor unit to exactly one posting.
